narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70 and an LSAT score of 37, you've clearly worked hard to build a strong academic foundation, coming from an upper-middle-class family and planning to attend law school full-time. The admissions model we use evaluates applicants based on how likely they are to pass the bar exam on the first try, drawing from patterns in data about thousands of similar students. For you specifically, the model predicts a 45.9% chance of failing the bar exam, which is high enough that it tipped the decision toward rejecting your application to protect the school's outcomes and bar passage rates. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is much lower than the typical student's rank around the middle of their class and signaled potential challenges in sustaining performance. This outweighed your strong undergraduate GPA, which was above the average of 3.21 and pulled strongly in favor of predicting success. Similarly, your LSAT score edged above the dataset average of 36.70, giving another meaningful boost toward a passing prediction. Your Year 1 decile of 1 also contributed toward the failure side, though less intensely than Year 3, suggesting early struggles that carried some weight. Factors like your upper-middle family income and full-time enrollment status helped a bit by aligning with profiles of successful students, but they weren't decisive enough to shift the balance. Compared to typical applicants, your undergrad record and LSAT shine brighter than average, but your lower decile rankings in school years stand out as weaker than most, driving the overall concern. While this rejection stings, especially with your strengths in key areas, it highlights where targeted improvement could make a real difference. I encourage you to consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, perhaps seeking tutoring or study strategies that address any hurdles from Years 1 and 3. Reapplying after strengthening those areas, or exploring schools with more flexible criteria, could turn this around—many students like you succeed with persistence. We're rooting for your next steps.
